scriptId: cs_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Relationship with a boss or coworker
historyId: h_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Relationship with a boss or coworker

goal: Handle the situation with the manager without losing my job or making the situation worse.
constraints: avoid job loss or career risk
Escalate promptly if harassment, discrimination, retaliation, or harm to job/compensation occurs.
Maintain a neutral, outcome-focused tone in communications
decisions: Protect your performance and reputation
Document interactions and contributions
Make your work visible, proactively
Manage meetings and interruptions
Prepare for a one-on-one with your manager
Reduce micromanagement with clear status and boundaries
Build allies and sponsors
Escalate only if necessary and carefully
Take care of yourself and have contingency plans
Draft neutral one-page timeline for the skip-level meeting.
Create and use an incident log template to document interruptions and miscrediting
Talk to trusted teammates to corroborate patterns and seek alignment
Consider a discreet skip-level conversation with your manager's manager to raise alignment and visibility concerns
Present documented facts, attempted remedies, and desired outcome when contacting HR
Clarify HR's role, confidentiality limits, and available options (mediation, coaching, formal complaint, accommodations)
Use email recap template after meetings to document agreements and actions.
Call out owners in cross-team emails when appropriate
Schedule a three-week recap check-in to assess effectiveness of post-meeting recaps
Present concrete business impact with evidence and time-boxed requests
Pursue a skip-level alignment meeting with the manager's manager when necessary.
Prepare a concise one-page timeline for the skip-level meeting.
Propose a RACI for key deliverables to discuss in the skip-level meeting.
Frame the skip-level request as alignment on priorities and ownership, not as a personal complaint.
Attach the timeline/recap when requesting the skip-level alignment.
Evaluate whether to invite a witness for private one-on-one meetings and, if so, obtain buy-in and define scope of observer role
Draft a post-meeting follow-up note template after one-on-one meetings to document agreements and maintain rapport
open: interruption of meetings by manager
manager taking credit for ideas
micromanagement of team by manager
morale impact due to manager behavior
visibility impact due to manager behavior
contributions omitted in cross-team emails
accusation of defensiveness or uncooperative behavior during meetings
Draft and send a recap after meetings listing owners and decisions
Check back after three weeks to evaluate if visibility or ownership improved
Call out owners in cross-team emails when appropriate
Request skip-level alignment meeting with manager's manager
Prepare a concise one-page timeline for the skip-level meeting.
Bring documentation and proposed fixes (recap emails, RACI) to skip-level meeting.
Let me finish updates without interruption; target fewer than 1 interruption per update over the next 4 meetings.
Add a 'Contributors / Owners' line to cross-team emails; target 90% of cross-team recaps include contributors over the next quarter.
Circulate brief agendas with timeboxed items 24 hours before meetings; target agendas sent 24 hours prior for 80%+ of recurring meetings during a 6-week trial.
Send a 24-hour post-meeting recap listing decisions and owners; target 100% of project meetings with a recap within 24 hours for the next month.
Limit ad-hoc status checks; use scheduled check-ins unless a clear trigger occurs; reduce unscheduled check-ins by 50% over 4 weeks.
Follow a RACI for key deliverables and respect assigned roles; complete RACI for top 6 deliverables and use as single source of truth for 2 months.
Publicly acknowledge lead contributors in announcements; target 90% of milestone announcements list lead contributors next quarter.
Discuss and confirm before reassigning work or changing approach; target zero uncommunicated reassignments over 6 weeks.
Provide feedback in one-on-ones rather than public forums; target zero public corrective feedback; document private feedback in one-on-one.
Consider requesting a witness in private one-on-one meetings and clarify scope with manager and HR; ensure observer notes are factual and non-confrontational.
